edges.description: Loss of ceruloplasmin ferroxidase activity causes progressive tissue iron deposition.
Ceruloplasmin loss is reflected clinically by very low serum ceruloplasmin levels.
Low serum copper accompanies severe ceruloplasmin loss because most circulating copper is ceruloplasmin-bound.
Ceruloplasmin-dependent iron export failure presents clinically with low transferrin saturation despite tissue iron loading.
intermediate_mechanisms: impaired cellular iron export and iron sequestration

edges.description: Systemic iron dysregulation produces early microcytic anemia.
Retinal iron deposition contributes to progressive retinopathy.
Pancreatic iron deposition contributes to diabetes mellitus.
Cerebral iron deposition drives the neurologic disease branch.
Multi-organ iron deposition is directly reflected by increased tissue iron burden.
Systemic iron misdistribution and storage are reflected by high serum ferritin.
intermediate_mechanisms: increased iron storage and ferritin release

edges.description: Brain iron accumulation contributes to cognitive decline.
Brain iron accumulation contributes to gait and postural dysfunction.
Brain iron accumulation is measured by characteristic MRI susceptibility abnormalities.
Low serum ceruloplasmin directly reports the CP/ceruloplasmin deficiency state.
Low serum copper tracks with severe loss of circulating ceruloplasmin.
Elevated ferritin reports systemic iron storage and iron misdistribution in aceruloplasminemia.
Low transferrin saturation reflects impaired ceruloplasmin-dependent iron mobilization despite tissue iron loading.
Increased tissue iron burden reports the central multi-organ iron accumulation mechanism.
MRI-visible brain iron reports the neurologic iron-accumulation branch.

evidence.snippet: Brain MRI revealed significant iron accumulation in multiple brain regions, including the dentate nuclei, cerebellar cortex, basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem nuclei, and hypothalamus.

readouts.interpretation: MRI-visible brain iron reports the neurologic iron-accumulation branch.

nodes.description: Progressive iron deposition in deep gray matter and cerebellar structures drives the neurologic manifestations of aceruloplasminemia.

evidence.snippet: Brain MRI revealed significant iron accumulation in multiple brain regions, including the dentate nuclei, cerebellar cortex, basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem nuclei, and hypothalamus.

Background and Clinical Significance: Aceruloplasminemia (ACP), a member of the neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) spectrum of disorders, is a rare disorder caused by mutations in the ceruloplasmin (CP) gene.
CP | HGNC:2295 | aceruloplasminemia | MONDO:0011426 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Loss of ceruloplasmin impairs ferroxidase-dependent iron handling and disrupts normal copper and iron metabolism.

evidence.snippet: Ceruloplasmin gene encodes ceruloplasmin protein, which has ferroxidase activity and is involved in copper and iron metabolism.

nodes.description: Cognitive decline can emerge as part of the neurologic phenotype in patients with progressive brain iron deposition.

evidence.snippet: Here, we report the first case of ACP in Greece. Case Presentation: Our patient was a 53-year-old male who was referred to our movement disorders center for a 6-month history of mild, unspecific, episodic dizziness and postural instability, and attention and memory deficits.

nodes.description: Pancreatic iron deposition can contribute to diabetes mellitus later in the disease course.

evidence.snippet: Progressive neurotoxicity, retinopathy, and diabetes may develop in about 40-60 decades.
Patients with aceruloplasminemia often present with microcytic anemia, low transferrin saturation ratio, and high serum ferritin levels before the onset of symptoms.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated ferritin reports systemic iron storage and iron misdistribution in aceruloplasminemia.

nodes.description: Iron chelation is used to reduce systemic and neurologic iron-related toxicity.

evidence.snippet: In particular, deferiprone is currently being exploited to treat several NBIA diseases (Vroegindeweij et al., 2020, Klopstock et al., 2019).

nodes.description: Persistent microcytic anemia is often an early clinical clue and can precede the later neurologic syndrome.

evidence.snippet: In addition, microcytic anemia accompanied by high ferritin and low ceruloplasmin level that develop at earlier ages can be first manifestation.

nodes.description: Impaired ceruloplasmin-dependent iron handling causes progressive iron deposition in the brain, liver, pancreas, and retina.

evidence.snippet: Aceruloplasminemia inherited autosomal recessively in the ceruloplasmin gene is a progressive disease with iron accumulation in various organs such as the brain, liver, pancreas, and retina.

nodes.description: Postural instability can emerge as part of the movement-disorder spectrum in patients with brain iron accumulation.

evidence.snippet: Here, we report the first case of ACP in Greece. Case Presentation: Our patient was a 53-year-old male who was referred to our movement disorders center for a 6-month history of mild, unspecific, episodic dizziness and postural instability, and attention and memory deficits.

nodes.description: Progressive retinal involvement develops as part of the later disease course.

evidence.snippet: Progressive neurotoxicity, retinopathy, and diabetes may develop in about 40-60 decades.
Anemia was accompanied by microcytosis, high ferritin, and low copper and ceruloplasmin levels.

readouts.interpretation: Low serum ceruloplasmin directly reports the CP/ceruloplasmin deficiency state.

evidence.snippet: Anemia was accompanied by microcytosis, high ferritin, and low copper and ceruloplasmin levels.
Subsequent testing revealed extremely low serum CP and low serum copper.

readouts.interpretation: Low serum copper tracks with severe loss of circulating ceruloplasmin.

evidence.snippet: Aceruloplasminemia inherited autosomal recessively in the ceruloplasmin gene is a progressive disease with iron accumulation in various organs such as the brain, liver, pancreas, and retina.

readouts.interpretation: Increased tissue iron burden reports the central multi-organ iron accumulation mechanism.

evidence.snippet: Patients with aceruloplasminemia often present with microcytic anemia, low transferrin saturation ratio, and high serum ferritin levels before the onset of symptoms.

readouts.interpretation: Low transferrin saturation reflects impaired ceruloplasmin-dependent iron mobilization despite tissue iron loading.
